coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots supported AgI /ZnO/phosphorus doped graphitic carbon nitride as Z-scheme photocatalyst for efficient photodegradation of 2, 4-dinitrophenol
coredata.dc:creator: Hasija, Vasudha
Sudhaik, Anita
Raizada, Pankaj
Hosseini-Bandegharaei, Ahmad
Singh, Pardeep
coredata.dc:description: The unique attributes of CQDs, such as low cost, facile synthesis, tremendous photochemical, and physicochemical stability, render them as versatile nanocomposites, as compared to conventional semiconductors. Herein, a green synthesis approach was opted using naturally occurring bamboo leaves as precursors for CQDs production via a facile and efficient hydrothermal method. The fabricated CQDs were exploited for construction of Z-scheme P-doped g-C3N4/AgI/ZnO/CQDs (PGCN) heterojunction by immobilizing AgI/ZnO/CQD on graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), that acts as support targeted for 2, 4-dinitrophenol (DNP) eradication from simulated wastewater. TEM image depicts the presence of different fringes on an agglomerated structure which confirms the formation of nanocrystalline heterostructure. XRD, XPS, FT-IR, and EDX were used to provide structural, morphological information of synthesized samples relating the existence of heterostructure between AgI and ZnO/CQDs with the sheet-like structured g-C3N4 which have undergone successful inclusion of P dopant into g-C3N4 framework. Congruously, the optical properties of modified photocatalyst with suppression in photogenerated electron-hole pair recombination g-C3N4/AgI/ZnO/CQD nanostructure were evidenced by photoluminescence (PL) spectra and UV–Vis diffused reflection spectra (UV–Vis DRS). Synergistic process of adsorption and photocatalysis was effectual for complete mineralization of DNP into CO2, H2O, and inorganic ions. The integration of g-C3N4 to AgI/ZnO with CQDs accelerated photocatalytic activity attributed to electron sink behavior of CQDs. The fabricated Z-scheme P-doped g-C3N4/AgI/ZnO/CQDs photocatalyst exhibited remarkable stability and recyclability for ten consecutive catalytic cycles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: AgI/ZnO
Carbon quantum dots
Z-scheme photocatalyst
Enhanced photocatalysis
2,4 dinitrophenol degradation

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issfirst issn issnnorm issuelist itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of the photocatalytic activity of PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs
Morphology and phase structure composition of PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs
Photocatalytic activity of PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs for 2,4-dinitrophenol (DNP) degradation
Possible mechanism for the enhanced photocatalytic activity of PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (a) Bare GCN (b) PGCN (c), (d) ZnO/AgI, (e) and (f) PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs.
EDX of (a) GCN, (b) PGCN, (c) AgI/ZnO and (d) PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs.
XRD pattern of GCN, PGCN, AgI/ZnO and PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs.
XPS of (a) Ag, (b) I, (c) P, (d) C, (e) N, (f) Zn and (g) O elements present in PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs.
(a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs (b) Tauc’s plot of PGCN/ZnO/AgI and (c) PL spectra of PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs.

(a) Photo removal of DNP under various reaction conditions, (b) Photocatalytic removal of DNP by different photocatalysts, (c) COD and (d) CO2 estimation during mineralization process. Reaction conditions: [DNP] = 1 × 10−4 mol dm-3; [photocatalyst] =50 mg/100 mL; initial reaction pH = 4.0 and Light intensity =750 lx.

(a) Scavenging effect during degradation of DNP using (a) PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs and (b) PGCN/AgI/CQDs (c) PGCN/ZnO/CQDs, (d) HPLC analysis of DNP after 120 min of photocatalysis. Reaction conditions: [DNP] = 1 × 10−4 mol dm-3; [scavenger] = 1 × 10−4 mol dm-3 [photocatalyst] =50 mg/100 mL; initial reaction pH = 4.0 and Light intensity =750 lx.
Proposed photocatalytic mechanism of (a) PGCN/AgI, (b) PGCN/ZnO and (c) PGCN/AgI/ZnO quaternary nanocomposite for DNP degradation.
(a) FTIR spectra of PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs after ten cycles, (b) Recycle efficiency of PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs.

The rate constant for DNP degradation under LED visible light. Reaction conditions: [DNP] = 1 × 10−4 mol dm-3; [catalyst] =50 mg/100 mL; initial reaction pH = 4.0; Time =8 h and light intensity =750 lx.
A comparative study of photodegradation of 2,4 dinitrophenol pollutant.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots supported AgI /ZnO/phosphorus doped graphitic carbon nitride as Z-scheme photocatalyst for efficient photodegradation of 2, 4-dinitrophenol

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, Faculty of Basic Sciences, Shoolini University, Solan HP 173229, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental Health Engineering, Faculty of Health, Sabzevar University of Medical Sciences, Sabzevar, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Engineering, Kashmar Branch, Islamic Azad University, PO BOx 161, Kashmar, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The unique attributes of CQDs, such as low cost, facile synthesis, tremendous photochemical, and physicochemical stability, render them as versatile nanocomposites, as compared to conventional semiconductors. Herein, a green synthesis approach was opted using naturally occurring bamboo leaves as precursors for CQDs production via a facile and efficient hydrothermal method. The fabricated CQDs were exploited for construction of Z-scheme P-doped g-C3N4/AgI/ZnO/CQDs (PGCN) heterojunction by immobilizing AgI/ZnO/CQD on graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), that acts as support targeted for 2, 4-dinitrophenol (DNP) eradication from simulated wastewater. TEM image depicts the presence of different fringes on an agglomerated structure which confirms the formation of nanocrystalline heterostructure. XRD, XPS, FT-IR, and EDX were used to provide structural, morphological information of synthesized samples relating the existence of heterostructure between AgI and ZnO/CQDs with the sheet-like structured g-C3N4 which have undergone successful inclusion of P dopant into g-C3N4 framework. Congruously, the optical properties of modified photocatalyst with suppression in photogenerated electron-hole pair recombination g-C3N4/AgI/ZnO/CQD nanostructure were evidenced by photoluminescence (PL) spectra and UV–Vis diffused reflection spectra (UV–Vis DRS). Synergistic process of adsorption and photocatalysis was effectual for complete mineralization of DNP into CO2, H2O, and inorganic ions. The integration of g-C3N4 to AgI/ZnO with CQDs accelerated photocatalytic activity attributed to electron sink behavior of CQDs. The fabricated Z-scheme P-doped g-C3N4/AgI/ZnO/CQDs photocatalyst exhibited remarkable stability and recyclability for ten consecutive catalytic cycles.

ce:section.ce:para: Bizarre industrialization, economic growth, and urbanization coupled with aggravating misuse of resources have resulted in critical deficits of water supplies. These alarming anthropogenic activities are posing peril to mankind and environment. For a progressive change in the domain of pollution alleviation, the challenge is to develop nanomaterial engineered and renewable solar energy driven paradigm water treatment technology. For this purpose, after several years of utilization of various advanced oxidation processes (AOPs), the ‘Honda Fujima effect’ commenced the era of semiconductor-based heterogeneous photocatalysis with TiO2 [1,2]. The mechanistic insight of photocatalysis process involves photoinduced generation of strong oxidants (h+) in the valence band (VB) and reductants (e−) in the conduction band (CB) via light absorption by a semiconductor [3]. The sequential interfacial charge transfer releases various reactive oxygen species (ROS), i.e. hydroxyl radicals (OH·), superoxide (O2 −), and peroxide (O2·) which participate in the degradation of organic and inorganic pollutants [4]. The prerequisite criteria for an efficient photocatalyst are appropriate band gap and optimized charge carrier separation under solar light illumination [5]. Conventional photocatalyst ZnO possess large band gap (3.37 eV), higher surface area and increased electron trapping sites due to native defects arising from oxygen non-stoichiometry. However, it absorbs only 4% radiation of the solar spectrum due to wide bandgap potential and, therefore, has restricted practical applicability [6]. In search of a robust and cost-effective semiconductor to counter the water energy demands, a metal-free n-type semiconductor graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) with a tunable band gap (2.7 eV), and good thermal and chemical stability, has become a hot-spot for large scale water purification applications [7]. Nevertheless, high recombination rate of photoexcitons and small specific surface area of pure g-C3N4 have been the lingering obstacles which lower the photocatalytic efficiency [8]. In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have garnered incredible fascination among scientific communities on account of its hierarchical properties including an amenable exterior surface, low toxicity, negligible aqueous solubility, and chemically inert biocompatible nature [9–11]. Furthermore, they possess inherent emission characteristics; stable fluorescence, electro-chemiluminescence and tunable photoluminescence (PL) [12]. These key advantages offer potential applications in various realms of photocatalysis, bioimaging, chemical sensors, and targeted drug delivery [13]. They are sp3 hybridized ᴨ-ᴨ conjugated of amorphous nature with significant amount of consideration owed to size requirement of CQDs as they are less than 10 nm zero dimensional structures. Researchers have developed various facile, cost-effective synthesis techniques and broadly classified, into top-down and bottom-up approaches [14]. In relation to above-mentioned advantages, CQDs act as electron bridge mediator for enhancement of overall performance of Z-scheme involving migration of electrons from SCI (AgI) to SC-III (ZnO) thereby, facilitating EHP recombination [15,16]. To date, various advancement strategies are explored to address the limitations offered by semiconductors, among them heterojunction and doping opted in this work [17,18]. From previously reported data it is evident that incorporation of non-metal dopant phosphorus (P) into g-C3N4 successfully increases specific surface area thus providing more reactive sites for enhanced visible light quantum absorption, thereby improving pollutant eradication [19,20]. Another attractive method is the construction of hybridized substances via coupling of composites. Xu et al. designed a novel visible light responsive g-C3N4 based heterojunction by coupling silver halide, AgX (X = Br, I), for effective removal of target pollutant 2, 4-dinitrophenol (DNP) [21]. Although silver iodide (AgI) photosensitive semiconductor has a narrow band gap (2.73 eV), it dramatically improves photodegradation activity in binary composite g-C3N4/AgI due to strong interactions which facilitated photon transfer and inhibited rapid electron hole pair (EHP) recombination [22]. However, its low specific surface area decreases the catalytic reactive sites; and difficulty in recyclability limits its practical large-scale application [23]. Undoubtedly, all the three photocatalysts possess outstanding photodegradation ability however, there heterojunction is made more feasible with CQDs acting as an electron mediator.

Phenols and their derivate compounds are used as components of dyes, drugs, and polymers, also released during production and degradation of pesticides. They exhibit peroxidative activity and are hematotoxin and hepatotoxic in nature which thereby induces carcinogenesis towards living organisms [24]. A phenolic pollutant, 2,4- Dinitrophenol (DNP) has been regarded as priority pollutant because of its carcinogenic and bioaccumulation in water bodies [25]. The removal of DNP is complicated due to its highly stable and resistant to degradation nature [26]. Herein, a Z-scheme assisted quaternary photocatalyst PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQD was successfully fabricated via the hydrothermal method with bamboo leaves. The obtained heterostructure nanocomposite possesses engineered textural surface, enhanced optical and electronic properties and tailorable band gap for the effective photodegradation of DNP as a toxic pollutant.

All chemicals employed were of analytical grade and purchased from Sigma Aldrich, India, and applied without further purification. The chemicals used are diammonium hydrogen phosphate (NH4)2HPO4, urea (NH2CONH2), ethanol (C2H5OH), silver iodide (AgI), zinc nitrate tetra hydrate Zn(NO3)2, silver nitrate (AgNO3), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), sodium iodide (NaI), zinc oxide (ZnO) and distilled water was used through-out for all experiments.

The porous phosphorus doped g-C3N4 were synthesized by condensation of precursors, i.e. urea and diammonium hydrogen phosphate as phosphorus source, under high temperature in a muffle furnace. In detail, 20 g of urea was dissolved in 50 mL ethanol to which 1.2 g of diammonium hydrogen phosphate was added. The reaction mixture was subject to ultrasonication for 10 min and then kept in an oven at 80 °C for evaporation of ethanol. The resulting solid precipitates were placed in a porcelain crucible with a cover and calcined at 550 °C for 2 h with a heating rate of 5 °C min−1. The obtained yellow product was grounded in a mortar into fine grains for further use and labeled as phosphorus doped graphitic carbon nitrite (PGCN). g-C3N4 (GCN) was synthesized following the same above procedure utilizing urea in the absence of diammonium hydrogen phosphate.

Typically, for the fabrication of binary nanocomposite, 0.44 g of silver iodide, 0.56 g of zinc nitrate tetra hydrate and 0.082 g of silver nitrate were dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water and kept for ultrasonication for 10 min at ambient temperature conditions. To the reaction mixture, 5 M NaOH was added drop-wise with constant stirring to attain optimum alkaline pH (9.5). To the obtained light brown mixture an aqueous solution of silver iodide (0.076 g in 50 mL of water) was added which led to change of color from brown to yellow. The olive color precipitates were collected and rinsed two times with deionized water and ethanol. Subsequently, the washed precipitates were dried at 60 °C for 24 h and further subjected to centrifugation.

For the fabrication of CQDs, bamboo leaves were taken from Shoolini University, Solan, India. In this green experiment, the hydrothermal method was employed in which 2 g of crushed bamboo leaves were cleaned, dried and added to 10 mL deionized water in a 25 mL beaker and kept on a hot plate for 3 h [27]. The obtained yellowish green mixture was crushed to powder form for further use.

Typical process involved drop-wise addition of 1 g zinc oxide to 1 g of silver iodide with constant stirring for 15 min followed by addition of 10 mL CQDs and the reaction mixture was subject to ultrasonication for 60 min. To the prepared reaction mixture, 1 g of PGCN was added and, then, again kept for ultrasonication for 15 min. The synthesized composite was oven-dried at 60 °C and washed with deionized water. The resultant final precipitate was labeled as PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs heterostructure nanocomposite (Scheme 1 ).

The morphological features were estimated via scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). SEM micrographs obtained using JEOL JSM- 6100 L V microscope at 30 kV and TEM images were recorded from model FP/5022-Tecnai G220S-TWIN (USA) applied at a voltage of 200 kV. The characteristic functional groups were analyzed by Perkin Elmer Fourier transformed (FTIR) spectrophotometer equipped with ATR having diamond crystal and ZnSe as focusing element. The crystal structure of fabricated ternary photocatalyst was characterized by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, PANalyticl’s X’Pert Pro) with Cu-Kα-1 (45 kV/100 mA). UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were obtained on a Perkin-Elmer Lambda 35 UV–vis spectrometer using BaSO4 as a reference. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured on PHI Versa Probe II with AES using 24.63 W Al Kα radiations. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of samples were chronicled on FLS-920 (Edinburgh Instrument). The degradation by-products during mineralization process were identified via high performance liquid chromatography analysis (Water HPLC, Austria) using Rheodyne manual injector kit and C18 column (5 μm, 25 cm length and 7 mm diameter). 10% of methanol was employed as eluent during the procedure. The LCMS spectra were recorded on JEOL GCMATE II GC-MS with a high-resolution data system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the photocatalytic activity of PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The photocatalytic activity of synthesized PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs was examined for degradation of DNP pollutant. The experiment was conducted in a photochemical slurry type batch reactor with doubled walled pyrex vessel of dimension of 7.5 cm × 6 cm (height × diameter) with W LED lamp as a source of visible light radiation. The pyrex vessel was provided with cooling jacket to maintain 30 ± 0.30 ℃ [24]. With continuous stirring of mixture, 3 mL aliquot of reaction mixture was withdrawn at regular intervals, centrifuged and monitored. The concentration of DNP in suspension was measured by UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-3600 Shimadzu) at 270 nm. The degradation percentage was computed by the following equation:
(1)
D e g r a d a t i o n
%
=
A
0 -
A t
A 0
Where A0 and At are, respectively, the initial (t = 0) and the final absorbance of dye at the time 't' (min).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and phase structure composition of PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The SEM image of GCN and PGCN as shown in (Figs. 1 a and b) respectively revealed lamellar morphology of GCN and incase of PGCN. It is observed that GCN possesses an aggregated pebble like morphology, whereas in case of PGCN there is clear disposition of P dopant with a decreased surface area and scattered irregular pore size. Figs. 1c and d depict distinct features of flower and needle-like composition of AgI/ZnO [25]. The formation of quaternary photocatalyst, PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs, is presented in Figs. 1e and f. The smooth surface and porous nature are attributed to PGCN and a uniform distribution of rod-shaped CQDs can be observed. It is clearly indicated that there is agglomeration with protruding sharp edges which reflects the crystalline nature of the nanocomposite [26]. From TEM images surface morphology and size were estimated. The binary composite, as shown in Figs. 2 a and b, depicts agglomeration of spherical shaped AgI and ZnO having fringes at 0.35 and 0.26 nm which are well distributed in the center [27,28]. The nanocomposite image, as shown in Figs. 2c, 2d and 2e, revealed dark-black spots of CQDs on the smooth surface of g-C3N4. The distance between lattice fringes were measured as 0.37 nm of g-C3N4, which are in-accordance to standard reported values and with previously reported literature [29]. EDX analysis (Fig. 3 ) specified that AgI/ZnO binary composite is composed of O, Zn, Ag, and I weight ratios of 13.47, 55.19, 11.58, and 19.75%, respectively. The purity of the as-prepared ternary photocatalyst was confirmed via presence of elements C, O, Zn, Ag, and I with weight ratios of 5.91, 10.66, 62.98, 7.77, and 12.68%, respectively.

FTIR spectra shown in Fig. S1 provide plenteous structural information of fabricated PGCN /AgI/ZnO/CQDs quaternary photocatalyst. For g-C3N4 strong, absorption bands ranging from 1200-1650 cm−1 were assigned to stretching vibrations modes of tri-s-triazine which is significant of CN and C–N heterocycles. The sharp peak at 809 cm−1 corresponded to breathing mode (bending vibration) of triazine units in heptazine rings which confirmed the formation g-C3N4 [30]. A series of peaks in the range of 3150-3300 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration modes of OH bonds. A characteristic peak at 3289 cm−1 indexed to NH stretching group [31]. A small peak centered at 942 cm−1, which is due to incorporation of phosphorus dopant, determines the formation of P doped g-C3N4 [32]. The sharp absorption peak positioned at 437 cm−1 is attributed to AgI [33], whereas the one at 576 cm−1 is regarded as the characteristic peak of ZnO species [34]. Meanwhile, the surface functional groups present in carbon quantum dots have their own characteristic bonds in FTIR spectrum, such as stretching vibration band of CO (1631 cm−1) and the stretching vibration bands of C–O (1086 cm−1) which are consistent with the literature [29]. Also, the stretching and bending vibrations of CH, CC, and C–H are designated with three obvious absorption peaks centered at 2172, 1331, and 621 cm−1, respectively, which confirm the presence of alkyl and aryl groups [35,36].

To decipher the crystal structure and heterojunction formation, XRD patterns were analyzed and presented in Fig. 4 XRD patterns of binary composite (AgI/ZnO) which are positioned at 2θ values of 22°,23°,39°,42°, and 46° can be attributed to (100), (002), (110), (103), and (112) planes of AgI (Fig. S2b) [37]. A series of diffraction peaks at 31.6°, 34.2°, 36.1°, 47.3°, 56.4°, 62.7°, and 68.9° corresponded to (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), and (112) of ZnO, respectively (Fig. S2 c) [38]. Furthermore, a weaker diffraction peak at 12.45° and a stronger diffraction peak at 27° were observed. The diffraction peak at 12.45° was attributed to in-plane structural pattern of tri-s-triazine units, which was located at (100) peak. The peak at 27° was ascribed to interlayer stacking of the conjugated aromatic ring, which was indexed as (002) peak of g-C3N4. Notably, a single broad diffraction (002) peak at 19° was exhibited by CQDs [39].

Chemical composition and oxidation states of different elements were analyzed via XPS analysis. In fact, silver, iodine, phosphorus, carbon, nitrogen, zinc and oxygen were examined in PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite through XPS analysis. The sharp peaks of Ag 3d spectrum at 367.3 eV and 373.51 eV binding energies were assigned to Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2 peaks (Fig. 5 a) [40]. Fig. 5b displayed two typical peaks at 618.61 and 630.05 eV which were attributed to binding energies of I 3d3/2 and 3d5/2, respectively [41]. POC and PO bonds displayed peaks at 133.48 and 135.99 eV that were formed via the replacement of carbon in PCN lattice as shown in Fig. 5c [42]. XPS spectrum of C1 s was displayed in Fig. 5d and peaks positioned at 284.18, 288.08 and 289.88 eV binding energies were entrusted with sp2 CC bond, CO, and NCC bonds in PCN, respectively [43]. In Fig. 5e, the spectrum of N 1s displayed a peak at 397.6 eV resembled pyridiniC–N [44]. In ZnO, zinc possessed two peaks at 1023.2 eV (Zn-2p3/2), and 1043.4 eV (Zn-2p1/2) binding energy which corresponded to Zn2+ state and peak designated at 532 eV was related to O 1s of oxygen atom as presented in Figs. 5f and 5 g [45].

Optical absorption and band gap energy profiles were assessed by UV–vis diffuse reflectance of pure AgI, ZnO and PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs quaternary photocatalyst. After incorporation of P dopant and CQDs in the initially prepared binary composite, the optical absorption edge of the quaternary nanostructure displayed a distinct red shift on the right side relative to that of the pure binary composite as shown in Fig. 6 a. From the previous studies, it is reported that ZnO, AgI and PGCN exhibit their maximum absorption peaks at about 367.9, 459.2, and 496 nm, respectively [46,47]. It is observed that there is an increased absorption maximum for heterostructure exhibiting significant absorption in the visible spectrum. Tauc’s plots were used to find the band-gap energy of prepared photocatalysts and the formula for calculation is as follow [37–39]:
(2)
α h v = A
h v -
E g
n / 2
where index (n) has 1/2, 2, 3/2, and 3 value for allowed direct and indirect, forbidden direct and indirect electronic transitions, respectively. Symbol α indicates absorption coefficient and hν is photon energy in Tauc equation. The optical band gaps of ZnO, AgI and PGCN were calculated to be 3.37, 2.73 and 2.5 eV, respectively. The computed band gaps had congruity with the previously reported values (Fig. 6b) [37–39]. The EHP recombination rate of fabricated semiconductor photocatalyst was investigated under an excitation wavelength of 280 nm at room temperature through photoluminescence (PL) spectra. Fig. 6c depicts PL spectra of semiconductors AgI, ZnO, PGCN, and PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs which indicates direct charge carrier recombination. In general, the lower the PL intensity is, the higher the separation of EHP recombination is facilitated. The decrease in intensity follows the order ZnO > AgI > PGCN > PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs. These results show that the property of CQDs to act as electron sink contributes to restricted charge carrier separation [27,32,33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic activity of PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs for 2,4-dinitrophenol (DNP) degradation
ce:section.ce:para:
The photocatalytic performance of PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs was assessed by the photo-degrading of DNP under LED light beam. Before photocatalytic experiments, adsorption–desorption equilibrium between DNP and photocatalysts was obtained by stirring DNP solution for 60 min in the dark conditions. Fig. 7 a displays photocatalytic degradation efficiency of DNP over PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs, PGCN/AgI/CQDs, PGCN/ZnO/CQDs, PGCN, AgI, ZnO and GCN which was 98%, 63%, 62%, 50%, 48%, 46% and 40% respectively, after 140 min radiation time (Table 1 ). However, photolysis in the absence of photocatalyst had no significant impression on DNP decomposition. The photocatalytic experiments inferred that PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs was the best photocatalyst for DNP degradation, amongst the studied others. On the basis of Langmuir Hinshelwood model, kinetics of DNP photodegradation was explored using the following equations [48,49].
(3)
-
ln
C t
C 0
= k t
(4)
ln
C 0
C t
= k t
where C and C0 respectively denote DNP concentration at irradiation time t (min) and the concentration at t = 0, and k (min−1) describes apparent reaction rate constant for DNP degradation. The plots of ln(C0/Ct) against time (t) (Fig. 7b) were used to find DNP degradation. The correlation coefficients (R2; 0.98-0.95) revealed pseudo-first order kinetics of degradation process. PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs, PGCN/AgI/CQDs, PGCN/ZnO/CQDs, PGCN, AgI, ZnO and GCN respectively depicted rate constants of 0.093, 0.065, 0.064, 0.048, 0.047 and 0.045 and 0.040 min−1 The photocatalytic investigations clearly confirmed the highest photocatalytic activity of PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs among the mentioned series of as-synthesized photocatalysts. The integrated heterojunction construction among PGCN, AgI, ZnO, and CQDs fostered overall photocatalytic efficacy of photocatalyst for DNP degradation. The order of photocatalytic activity can be represented as: PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs (R2 = 0.96) > PGCN/AgI/CQDs (R2 = 0.97) PGCN/ZnO/CQDs(R2 = 0.96) > PGCN (R2 = 0.96) > AgI (R2 = 0.97) > ZnO (R2 = 0.98) > GCN (R2 = 0.97). Furthermore, COD and CO2 were estimated during the photodegradation process. 100% of COD removal was attainted in 240 min using PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs photocatalyst under LED light. Under the same reaction conditions, PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs, PGCN/AgI/CQDs, PGCN/ZnO/CQDs, PGCN, AgI, ZnO and GCN showed 52%, 42%, 31%, 27%, 26% and 24% of COD removal for DNP degradation as illustrated in Fig. 7c. COD removal experiments clearly suggested the complete degradation of DNP under chosen reaction conditions. COD removal was in accordance with CO2 estimation experiment. The CO2 formation during the degradation process indicates conversion of DNP into CO2 and H2O. The mineralization ability of PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs was higher in comparison of other used photocatalytic systems as revealed in (Fig. 7d). The enhanced photocatalytic activity of PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs was due to rapid separation of photoinduced charge carriers and extended visible light absorption.

To analyze the intermediates formed during photodegradation of DNP, high-performance liquid chromatography analysis (Water HPLC, Austria, C18 column, 5 μm, 25 cm length, and 7 mm diameter) was exploited. Acetic acid: methanol: water (1:39:60) ratio was taken as the eluent solution and was run at a flow rate of 0.5 mL min−1. The GC-MS results were analyzed on JEOL GCMATE II GC-MS with a high-resolution data system. The decrease in peak intensity was recorded between retention times of 2–4 min and finally dispersed after 240 min of reaction time. Furthermore, gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) investigation was performed using JEOL GCMATE II GC-MS equipped with C18 column (150 mm × 2 mm), using an injection volume of 20 μL. The LC-MS analysis was performed for the DNP sample after 120 min of photocatalysis under visible light irradiation. Moreover, the formation of intermediates was confirmed by exploiting gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS). The recorded peaks for DNP were at m/z ratios of 45 (formic acid), 88 (oxalic acid), 108 (benzoquinone), 110 (hydroquinone/resorcinol/catechol), 114 (maleic acid), and 116 (succinic acid). These observations are consistent with those reported the literature (Fig. S4) [50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism for the enhanced photocatalytic activity of PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: It is well documented that h+, ∙ O2, and ∙ OH are three principal oxidative species leading to the photodegradation of organic contaminants in simulated waters [51]. In this study, trapping experiments were conducted to find active species responsible for the degradation of DNP by PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs photocatalyst. Isopropanol (IPA), benzoquinone (BZQ), and disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (Na2-EDTA) were applied as scavengers for ∙ OH, ∙ O2, and h+, respectively [17,18]. Fig. 8 a depicts photodegradation of DNP with PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs photocatalyst both before and after the addition of trapping scavengers. It is seen that addition of BZQ and IPA decreased the photodegradation efficiency to 39% and 13%, respectively. Fig. 8b exhibits the influence of adding scavengers on the photocatalytic efficacy of photocatalyst. The results showed that, upon addition of IPA and BZQ, photodegradation efficiency of PGCN/AgI/CQDs was reduced to 9% and 30%, respectively. From the experimental studies, it is completely explicit that ∙ OH, h+, and ∙ O2 are the principal oxidizing species responsible for the photodegradation process. From Fig. 8c, it is noticed that Na2-EDTA had no significant effect on DNP degradation in case of PGCN/ZnO/CQD, while addition of IPA and BZQ resulted in 13% and 9% of DNP removal efficiency. It means that, during PCN assisted photocatalytic processes, both h+ and ∙O2 were the principal oxidizing species in DNP degradation. From scavenging experiment, the following trend was obtained for the effects of scavengers: IPA > BZQ > Na2-EDTA.

A possible proposed Z-scheme mechanism for photocatalytic degradation of DNP under visible light irradiation over PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs is based upon the band gap energies of the quaternary photocatalyst. The CB and VB energies for PGCN, AgI and ZnO were calculated using Tauc plot obtained from Eq. (5) and (6) [52,53]:
(5)
E
V B
= χ -
E e
+ 0.5
E g
(6)
E
C B
=
E
V B
-
E g
where Eg is the bandgap; EVB and ECB are, respectively, valence and conduction band energy, as shown in Fig. 9 . After exposure to LED light, EHPs are generated on the surface of PGCN, the CB potential of PGCN (-1.05 eV) is more negative than the potential of O2/· O2 − (-0.33 eV). Therefore, on the surface of PGCN, oxygen molecules are converted to ·O2 − [54]. The generated ·O2 − react with DNP to release simpler degraded products, i.e. CO2, H2O, and inorganic ions. Furthermore, photogenerated electrons from CB of AgI (-0.42 eV) and CB of ZnO (-0.34 eV) are transferred to VB of PGCN (+1.56 eV) via electron mediator CQDs as illustrated in (Fig. 9a and b) [55,56]. Simultaneously, CQDs act as an electron sink as well as a mediator in the photocatalytic system, following Z-scheme photocatalytic mechanism, which further enhances the photogenerated electron migration ability and, also, prolongs the lifespan of photoexcitons. In detail, CQDs under visible light radiations strongly retorts by the release of EHPs, and emits light of smaller wavelength due to its inherent properties [57]. The VB of ZnO (2.86 eV) and AgI (2.38 eV) has enough oxidation potential for oxidation of absorbed OH−/OH· (2.40 eV) for photodegradation of DNP. As a result, there is an equal contribution of photogenerated holes and electrons in the photodegradation of DNP pollutant. It is noteworthy that CQDs itself can absorb visible light even though it is deprived of photocatalytic abilities [58,62]. Moreover, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy analysis was performed to ascertain the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers. The lesser arc size results in lower charge-transfer resistance on the electrode surface, which has high rate of separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [19,66]. Figure S4 shows EIS spectra of PGCN, AgI, ZnO and PGCN/ AgI/ZnO/CQDs. The impedance curve had the following trend: PGCN > AgI > ZnO > PGCN/ AgI/ZnO/CQDs. The EIS spectra was well supported by photoluminescence study of prepared photocatalysts. For the purpose of large-scale applications, reusability and stability of photocatalyst are vital features [59,60–63]. To determine recyclability, PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs was separated d by centrifugation from the reaction solution and washed with distilled water, and preserved for further experimentation. The FTIR spectrum of PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs did not show any significant change after 10 ten catalytic cycles (Fig. 10 a). The photocatalytic activity of PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs remained unaltered for the first 4 consecutive cycles (Fig. 10b) [64]. 89% of recycling efficiency was observed over 10 consecutive cycles for DNP degradation. It indicated the stability of PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs photocatalysts during DNP degradation (Table 2 ).

In summary, a quaternary nanocomposite PGCN/AgI/ZnO/CQDs has been successfully fabricated by an efficient hydrothermal method using naturally occurring bamboo leaves as precursors for CQDs production, while excluding the use of toxic reducing agents. The synthesis strategy presented here offers a green and facile route for visible light driven and stable nonmetal doped photocatalyst. The phenolic pollutant (2, 4-dinitrophenol) degradation and mineralization mainly occurred via h+, ∙ OH and ∙ O2 oxidative species which was revealed by analysis of experimental data obtained in the presence of radical scavengers. The adsorption process followed pseudo first-order kinetics with 98% DNP photodegradation in 2 h of visible light irradiation. The versatility of CQDs is worth noting; it has dual photodegradation and electron mediator capabilities, thereby retarding the recombination of charge carriers as evidenced by PL spectra and mechanistic studies. Furthermore, 89% of efficiency was obtained over ten consecutive catalytic cycles, indicating the high recyclability of the photocatalyst.
None.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jece.2019.103272.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
